annotations.label: Simplex algorithm

anchor.snippet: is an algorithm for linear programming

annotations.note: Mathlib has an executable simplex-algorithm implementation, but only as a meta tactic oracle used by linarith for certificate search, not as a general LP solver with a mathematical specification.

annotations.label: Canonical form of a linear program

anchor.snippet: The simplex algorithm operates on linear programs in the canonical form

annotations.note: No mathematical definition of a linear program (canonical form: maximize cᵀx s.t. Ax ≤ b, x ≥ 0) is found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Basic feasible solution (vertex of feasible polytope)

anchor.snippet: An extreme point or vertex of this polytope is known as basic feasible solution

annotations.note: The general convex-geometric notion of extreme points exists (`Set.extremePoints`, `IsExtreme`), but no LP-specific algebraic notion of basic feasible solution is formalized.

annotations.label: Maximum attained at an extreme point

anchor.snippet: if the objective function has a maximum value on the feasible region, then it has this value on (at least) one of the extreme points

annotations.note: The Krein–Milman theorem is in Mathlib, but the LP/Bauer maximum-principle statement that a linear objective on a polytope attains its maximum at an extreme point is not stated as such.

annotations.label: Improving edge from a non-optimal extreme point

anchor.snippet: if an extreme point is not a maximum point of the objective function, then there is an edge containing the point so that the value of the objective function is strictly increasing on the edge moving away from the point

annotations.note: No formalization of polytope edges or of the local improvement lemma for the simplex method is found.

annotations.label: Termination of the simplex algorithm

anchor.snippet: The algorithm always terminates because the number of vertices in the polytope is finite

annotations.note: The linarith oracle's `runSimplexAlgorithm` is asserted to terminate by Bland's rule in a docstring, but no termination theorem is proved.

annotations.label: Infeasible linear program

anchor.snippet: In the latter case the linear program is called infeasible

annotations.note: Only an `infeasible` exception constructor in the tactic monad exists; there is no mathematical definition of infeasibility of an LP.

annotations.label: Phase I and Phase II of the simplex method

anchor.snippet: The solution of a linear program is accomplished in two steps

annotations.note: Two-phase simplex method is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Slack variable

anchor.snippet: a new variable, called a slack variable , is introduced to change the constraint to an equality constraint

annotations.note: Slack variables for LP are not formalized; no occurrence of the term in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Surplus variable

anchor.snippet: some authors refer to the variable introduced as a surplus variable

annotations.note: Surplus variables for LP are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard-form transformation procedure

anchor.snippet: The transformation of a linear program to one in standard form may be accomplished as follows

annotations.note: No LP standardization procedure is formalized.

annotations.label: Canonical form of a simplex tableau

anchor.snippet: then the tableau is said to be in canonical form

annotations.note: The `Tableau` structure stores basic/free indices and the coefficient matrix, but no formal predicate for `canonical form` is defined.

annotations.label: Basic and nonbasic (free) variables

anchor.snippet: The variables corresponding to the columns of the identity matrix are called basic variables while the remaining variables are called nonbasic or free variables

annotations.note: The `Tableau` structure carries `basic` and `free` index arrays, mirroring the partition into basic and nonbasic variables at the implementation level.

annotations.label: Reduction of any BFS to canonical-form tableau

anchor.snippet: given a basic feasible solution, the columns corresponding to the nonzero variables can be expanded to a nonsingular matrix

annotations.note: The statement that any BFS can be expanded to a canonical-form tableau via a nonsingular submatrix is not formalized.

annotations.label: Pricing out and relative cost coefficients

anchor.snippet: This process is called pricing out and results in a canonical tableau

annotations.note: Pricing out and reduced/relative cost coefficients are not named or formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pivot operation

anchor.snippet: The geometrical operation of moving from a basic feasible solution to an adjacent basic feasible solution is implemented as a pivot operation

annotations.note: `doPivotOperation` implements a pivot on the linarith oracle's `Tableau`, swapping one basic and one free index and updating the matrix, but lacks a mathematical specification.

annotations.label: Entering and leaving variables

anchor.snippet: the variable corresponding to the pivot column enters the set of basic variables and is called the entering variable

annotations.note: `chooseEnteringVar` and `chooseExitingVar` (combined in `choosePivots`) play the roles of entering/leaving variable selection in the tactic, without abstract definitions.

annotations.label: Entering variable selection rule

anchor.snippet: one should select a pivot column whose the corresponding entry in the objective row

annotations.note: Mathlib's `chooseEnteringVar` selects an entering variable, but using Bland's smallest-index rule rather than the Dantzig negative-cost rule described in the article.

annotations.label: Optimality when no negative entry in objective row

anchor.snippet: If none of the entries in the objective row is negative then no choice of entering variable can be made and the solution is in fact at the maximum

annotations.note: The optimality criterion for tableau form is not proven as a theorem.

annotations.label: Unboundedness criterion in leaving variable selection

anchor.snippet: If there are no positive entries in the pivot column then the entering variable can take any non-negative value with the solution remaining feasible

annotations.note: The unboundedness detection criterion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Minimum ratio test

anchor.snippet: This is called the minimum ratio test

annotations.note: `chooseExitingVar` computes the minimum ratio inside the linarith tactic, but the minimum ratio test is not defined abstractly.

annotations.label: Simplex example (Phase II run)

anchor.snippet: Consider the linear program

annotations.note: No worked LP example of this form appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Artificial variables and Phase I problem

anchor.snippet: This can be accomplished by the introduction of artificial variables

annotations.note: Artificial variables and the Phase I auxiliary LP are not formalized.

annotations.label: Phase I termination and feasibility characterization

anchor.snippet: The simplex algorithm applied to the Phase I problem must terminate with a minimum value for the new objective function

annotations.note: The Phase I termination/feasibility theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Phase I/II simplex example with equality constraints

anchor.snippet: It differs from the previous example by having equality instead of inequality constraints

annotations.note: No two-phase worked example appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard simplex algorithm (tableau implementation)

anchor.snippet: This implementation is referred to as the " standard simplex algorithm"

annotations.note: Mathlib's `runSimplexAlgorithm` operates on the explicit `Tableau` matrix, matching the tableau-based standard implementation, though only for linarith certificate search.

annotations.label: Revised simplex algorithm

anchor.snippet: These observations motivate the " revised simplex algorithm "

annotations.note: The revised simplex method (basis-inverse representation) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Degenerate basic feasible solution

anchor.snippet: Basic feasible solutions where at least one of the basic variables is zero are called degenerate

annotations.note: Degeneracy of a BFS is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stalling and cycling

anchor.snippet: Worse than stalling is the possibility the same set of basic variables occurs twice

annotations.note: Notions of stalling and cycling for the simplex method are not formalized.

annotations.label: Bland's rule prevents cycling

anchor.snippet: Bland's rule prevents cycling and thus guarantees that the simplex algorithm always terminates

annotations.note: Bland's rule is implemented in the linarith oracle (in `choosePivots`), but its no-cycling/termination guarantee is not proved as a theorem.

annotations.label: Klee–Minty exponential worst case

anchor.snippet: showing that the worst-case complexity of simplex method as formulated by Dantzig is exponential time

annotations.note: Klee–Minty's exponential lower bound is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: NP-mightiness of a simplex variant

anchor.snippet: it was proved

annotations.note: Complexity-theoretic results about simplex variants are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PSPACE-completeness of Dantzig's pivot rule output

anchor.snippet: computing the output of Dantzig's pivot rule is PSPACE-complete

annotations.note: PSPACE-completeness of simplex pivot output is not formalized.

annotations.label: Polynomial average-case complexity

anchor.snippet: The simplex algorithm has polynomial-time average-case complexity under various probability distributions

annotations.note: Average-case polynomial complexity of simplex is not formalized.

annotations.label: Smoothed analysis of the simplex method

anchor.snippet: the running time of the simplex method on input with noise is polynomial in the number of variables and the magnitude of the perturbations

annotations.note: Spielman–Teng smoothed analysis is not formalized.

annotations.label: Linear-fractional programming

anchor.snippet: the objective function of a linear–fractional program is a ratio of two linear functions

annotations.note: Linear-fractional programming is not formalized in Mathlib.
